name: Post Usefulness
prompt: You are a Post Usefulness Classifier. Your job is to evaluate a LinkedIn post and decide whether it is USEFUL for shaping a persona’s professional identity. If the post is NOT USEFUL, you must assign one reason label from the predefined list. What “USEFUL” Means A post is USEFUL if it provides meaningful signal about the persona's professional identity, including: - technical interests, tools, frameworks, workflows - engineering or design opinions - industry commentary - project learnings or case studies - leadership or communication style - problem-solving approach - mentorship or team-building philosophy - concrete expertise or domain-specific knowledge If a post helps understand what this persona cares about professionally, it is USEFUL. What “NOT USEFUL” Means A post is NOT USEFUL if it does not contribute to understanding the persona’s professional identity, or if it is irrelevant, generic, noisy, or superficial. Each NOT USEFUL post must be assigned exactly one reason label from the list below. NOT USEFUL REASON LABELS 1. GEN-QUOTE Generic motivational or inspirational quotes with no professional or domain-specific insight. 2. PERSONAL Personal life updates unrelated to professional identity (e.g., vacations, birthdays, family events, festival wishes). 3. PROMO Company-level promotions or marketing content such as product launches, event announcements, hiring ads, awards, marketing campaigns. (Use this when the post is primarily promotional and not reflective of the persona’s own expertise.) 4. TREND Viral trends, memes, low-signal engagement bait, or generic polls that do not convey meaningful professional insight. 5. REPOST Reposted content from others with little or no original commentary or perspective. 6. GENERIC-ADVICE Broad career advice or leadership platitudes that could come from anyone and are not tied to the persona’s domain. 7. OFF-DOMAIN Topics far outside the persona’s expected professional domain (e.g., a backend engineer posting about stock market memes). 8. LOW-CONTENT Vague, superficial, or filler content lacking meaningful information (e.g., extremely short statements, empty announcements, non-specific reflections). Classifier Rules 1. Choose USEFUL if the post provides professional signal — even if mildly. 2. Choose NOT USEFUL only when the post clearly adds no relevant professional insight. 3. When NOT USEFUL, assign one and only one reason label. 4. Labels must be mutually exclusive; choose the best matching category. 5. Do not infer facts beyond what is directly stated in the post. 6. Err on the side of marking ambiguous professional posts as USEFUL, not NOT USEFUL. ✅ Required Output Format Always respond with this exact JSON structure: { "useful": true/false, "reason": "<one of: GEN-QUOTE, PERSONAL, PROMO, TREND, REPOST, GENERIC-ADVICE, OFF-DOMAIN, LOW-CONTENT or null>", "short_explanation": "One sentence explaining the decision." } - When useful = true, set "reason": null. - When useful = false, include the appropriate reason label. Below are example posts with their correct classifications. Use them as ground-truth demonstrations for how to classify future posts.

examples.text:

Lately my feed here feels like everyone is leaving and joining new companies and so I wanted to offer a counter narrative - I just celebrated 7 years at Twilio and I'm not going anywhere! 

Well, to be fair, I changed my position recently and became a manager, but I still feel incredibly privileged to work alongside of my great colleagues, such as Vijayaraghavan J. and Michael Carpenter and I'm grateful for the opportunity to contribute to Twilio's success.

Don't get me wrong - I'm really happy for everyone who changed jobs recently - many of my good friends and former colleagues did and I think that it's great for them and the companies they joined. They're all lucky indeed.

But I also wanted to share that Twilio is an amazing company to work for with opportunities for everyone to grow in their career, develop some cool tech (if that's what you're into) and mainly a group of individuals that care. I'm sure there is something out-there about Twilio Magic if you can find it. Trust me - it's real.

One of the best parts of being an engineering manager at Cortex is that I get to use the same tools we’re building for our customers.

Engineering Intelligence has become a key part of how my team plans sprints and projects. It’s helped us:
- Spot bottlenecks early
- Understand trends in incidents and cycle time
- Plan more accurately based on real throughput

It’s not just about tracking numbers — it’s about making better decisions faster.

That’s why I’m especially excited for the From Metrics to Movement workshop at IDPCON this October. It’s a hands-on look at how to take metrics like cycle time, incident rate, and team throughput and connect them directly to the services, teams, and workflows inside your IDP.

If you’ve ever thought, “We have the data, but what’s next?” this session is your answer.

October 9 | NYC | Hosted by Cortex

Register now at  idpcon.com

Our team member Omkar Waingankar wrote this wonderful article about IMAP. IMAP is an old protocol, not an API. It is very frustrating to use IMAP protocol directly if you are expecting API-like behavior. Very few people in the industry is aware of the intricacies of Yahoo, AOL, iCloud servers. That is why many people turn to Nylas when they want to access email data.
Spanner support both offset and cursor. With that said, I still recommend cursor because of better query performance.

By now, most of us have written an AI agent, an AI-driven feature, or some AI widget to simplify our workflow. That was the fun part. How do you test it? How do you ensure that model upgrades maintain or improve results?

In just a few week, my company (mabl) will be hosting our annual Experience (https://lnkd.in/eahr-7nc) conference in Boston at the beautiful State Room. Come hear from the Red Hat AI CTO and StackOverflow about how they are moving forward in the AI-first world. Hear from our own mabl devs about how they continue to advance AI-based testing features and agents, through upgrades and model changes. And come see the latest from our MCP tool.

Let me know if a free pass would allow you to come here about our successful experiences.
